addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Entered plan mode. You should now focus on exploring the codebase and designing an implementation approach.

In plan mode, you should:
1. Thoroughly explore the codebase to understand existing patterns
2. Identify similar features and architectural approaches
3. Consider multiple approaches and their trade-offs
4. Use AskUserQuestion if you need to clarify the approach
5. Design a concrete implementation strategy
6. When ready, use ExitPlanMode to present your plan for approval

Remember: DO NOT write or edit any files yet. This is a read-only exploration and planning phase.

content.content:
# GitHub Issue #122: wasa session resume Bug Report

## Title
"wasa session resume forks a new session but leaves the source paused"

## Core Problem
The `wasa session resume` command creates a duplicate session record instead of reviving the original. When resuming a paused session, users end up with two registry entries for the same branch: a new running session plus the original remaining in "paused" state, allowing the same source to be resumed repeatedly.

## Expected Behavior
After resuming, the source session should transition from paused to exited status and disappear from the resumable pool. Only one running session should exist per branch.

## Root Cause
The CLI implementation in `internal/cli/resume.go` calls `launch.CreateSession()` to mint a new session but never updates the source session's status. In contrast, the TUI path (`launch.ResumeSession`) rebuilds in place without creating duplicates.

## Proposed Solution
After successful session creation, mark the source as exited before saving the registry:

```
reg.MarkExited(src.ID)
```

This leverages existing functionality to cleanly transition the source from paused to exited status while preserving lineage through the `ResumedFrom` field.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Source no longer appears as resumable after resume completes
- Exactly one running session exists per branch
- Resuming already-exited sessions works without error
- `resumedFrom` lineage is preserved
- Help text documents the retirement behavior

# GitHub Issue #122: wasa session resume Bug Report

## Title
"wasa session resume forks a new session but leaves the source paused"

## Core Problem
The `wasa session resume` command creates a duplicate session record instead of reviving the original. When resuming a paused session, users end up with two registry entries for the same branch: a new running session plus the original remaining in "paused" state, allowing the same source to be resumed repeatedly.

## Expected Behavior
After resuming, the source session should transition from paused to exited status and disappear from the resumable pool. Only one running session should exist per branch.

## Root Cause
The CLI implementation in `internal/cli/resume.go` calls `launch.CreateSession()` to mint a new session but never updates the source session's status. In contrast, the TUI path (`launch.ResumeSession`) rebuilds in place without creating duplicates.

## Proposed Solution
After successful session creation, mark the source as exited before saving the registry:

```
reg.MarkExited(src.ID)
```

This leverages existing functionality to cleanly transition the source from paused to exited status while preserving lineage through the `ResumedFrom` field.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Source no longer appears as resumable after resume completes
- Exactly one running session exists per branch
- Resuming already-exited sessions works without error
- `resumedFrom` lineage is preserved
- Help text documents the retirement behavior

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

content.content:
1	package cli
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"flag"
6		"fmt"
7		"os"
8	
9		"github.com/joakimcarlsson/wasa-cli/internal/backend"
10		"github.com/joakimcarlsson/wasa-cli/internal/launch"
11		"github.com/joakimcarlsson/wasa-cli/internal/record"
12		"github.com/joakimcarlsson/wasa-cli/internal/registry"
13		"github.com/joakimcarlsson/wasa-cli/internal/worktree"
14	)
15	
16	const sessionResumeHelp = `usage: wasa session resume <session-id|branch>
17	
18	Resume a finished or paused session: recreate its worktree on the recorded
19	branch, relaunch its agent and continue from the recorded context rather than
20	starting cold. <session-id|branch> is a session id or title (a unique id prefix
21	also works), or the branch a recorded session worked on; the newest session on
22	that branch is chosen.
23	
24	The agent continues natively when it supports resuming a prior session (its
25	transcript is reused, restored from the checkpoint if the local copy is gone);
26	otherwise it is launched with a compact preamble derived from the checkpoint
27	(intent, commits produced, recent conversation), and the output says which
28	happened.
29	
30	The resumed session is a normal wasa session — registered, visible in the
31	cockpit and recorded again, with resumedFrom set to the original session id. The
32	recorded branch must still exist; a deleted branch is a clear error and nothing
33	is created.
34	`
35	
36	const sessionPauseHelp = `usage: wasa session pause [--force] <session>
37	
38	Pause a session: stop its tmux and remove its worktree, but keep its branch and
39	registry record so "wasa session resume" can rebuild it. <session> is a session
40	id or title (a unique id prefix also works).
41	
42	Flags:
43	  --force      pause even if the worktree has uncommitted or untracked changes
44	               (discards them); without it a dirty worktree blocks the pause
45	  -h, --help   show this help and exit
46	`
47	
48	func sessionResume(args []string) error {
49		fs := newFlagSet("wasa session resume")
50		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
51			if errors.Is(err, flag.ErrHelp) {
52				fmt.Fprint(os.Stdout, sessionResumeHelp)
53				return nil
54			}
55			return err
56		}
57		rest := fs.Args()
58		if len(rest) != 1 {
59			return errors.New("usage: wasa session resume <session-id|branch>")
60		}
61	
62		reg, _, err := openRegistry()
63		if err != nil {
64			return err
65		}
66	
67		src, err := resolveResumeTarget(reg, rest[0])
68		if err != nil {
69			return err
70		}
71		if src.Status == registry.StatusRunning {
72			return fmt.Errorf(
73				"session %s is running; attach to it instead of resuming", src.ID,
74			)
75		}
76		if src.Branch == "" {
77			return fmt.Errorf("session %s has no branch to resume", src.ID)
78		}
79		if src.WorkspaceID == "" {
80			return fmt.Errorf(
81				"session %s has no workspace to resume against", src.ID,
82			)
83		}
84		ws, ok := reg.Workspace(src.WorkspaceID)
85		if !ok {
86			return fmt.Errorf("workspace %s not found", src.WorkspaceID)
87		}
88	
89		wt := worktree.New(ws.RepoPath, wasaHome(), ws.ID)
90		if !wt.BranchExists(src.Branch) {
91			return fmt.Errorf(
92				"branch %s is gone; resume does not recreate branches from "+
93					"checkpoint data", src.Branch,
94			)
95		}
96		worktreePath := wt.Path(src.Branch)
97	
98		entry, intent, transcript, _ := record.Find(ws.RepoPath, src.ID)
99	
100		params := launch.Params{
101			Branch:      src.Branch,
102			Title:       src.Title,
103			Program:     src.Program,
104			Profile:     src.ProfileName,
105			ResumedFrom: src.ID,
106		}
107		if native := planResume(
108			src.Program, entry.Meta.AgentSessionID, worktreePath, transcript,
109		); native != nil {
110			params.ResumeArgs = native
111			fmt.Fprintf(
112				os.Stdout, "resuming %s natively (agent session %s)\n",
113				agentLabel(src.Program), entry.Meta.AgentSessionID,
114			)
115		} else {
116			params.InitialPrompt = record.BuildPreamble(
117				intent,
118				entry.Meta,
119				transcript,
120			)
121			fmt.Fprintln(
122				os.Stdout,
123				"resuming from checkpoint record, agent-native session not found",
124			)
125		}
126	
127		s, err := launch.CreateSession(wasaHome(), reg, ws, params)
128		if err != nil {
129			return err
130		}
131		if err := reg.Save(); err != nil {
132			return err
133		}
134	
135		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stdout, "resumed session %s from %s\n", s.ID, src.ID)
136		fmt.Fprintln(os.Stdout, s.TmuxName)
137		return nil
138	}
139	
140	// planResume decides how a resumed session continues: it returns the agent's
141	// native resume argv when the agent supports it and its transcript is available
142	// (present locally, or restorable from the checkpoint), or nil to fall back to a
143	// checkpoint-derived preamble.
144	func planResume(
145		program, agentSessionID, worktreePath string, transcript []byte,
146	) []string {
147		args, ok := record.ResumeArgs(program, agentSessionID)
148		if !ok {
149			return nil
150		}
151		if record.LocalTranscript(program, agentSessionID, worktreePath) != "" {
152			return args
153		}
154		if len(transcript) > 0 && record.RestoreTranscript(
155			program, agentSessionID, worktreePath, transcript,
156		) == nil {
157			return args
158		}
159		return nil
160	}
161	
162	// resolveResumeTarget finds the session to resume from a query that is either a
163	// session id/title (via resolveSession) or a branch name, in which case the
164	// newest session recorded on that branch is chosen. It returns resolveSession's
165	// error when neither matches, so an ambiguous id prefix is reported as such.
166	func resolveResumeTarget(
167		reg *registry.Registry,
168		query string,
169	) (*registry.Session, error) {
170		s, err := resolveSession(reg, query)
171		if err == nil {
172			return s, nil
173		}
174		var match *registry.Session
175		for _, sess := range reg.ListSessions() {
176			if sess.Branch == query &&
177				(match == nil || sess.CreatedAt.After(match.CreatedAt)) {
178				match = sess
179			}
180		}
181		if match != nil {
182			return match, nil
183		}
184		return nil, err
185	}
186	
187	func agentLabel(program string) string {
188		if tool, ok := record.AgentForProgram(program); ok {
189			return tool
190		}
191		return program
192	}
193	
194	func sessionPause(args []string) error {
195		fs := newFlagSet("wasa session pause")
196		var force bool
197		fs.BoolVar(
198			&force,
199			"force",
200			false,
201			"pause even if the worktree is dirty (discards uncommitted changes)",
202		)
203		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
204			if errors.Is(err, flag.ErrHelp) {
205				fmt.Fprint(os.Stdout, sessionPauseHelp)
206				return nil
207			}
208			return err
209		}
210		rest := fs.Args()
211		if len(rest) != 1 {
212			return errors.New("usage: wasa session pause [--force] <session>")
213		}
214	
215		reg, _, err := openRegistry()
216		if err != nil {
217			return err
218		}
219		s, err := resolveSession(reg, rest[0])
220		if err != nil {
221			return err
222		}
223		if err := launch.PauseSession(
224			reg, backend.Default(), wasaHome(), s, force,
225		); err != nil {
226			return err
227		}
228		if err := reg.Save(); err != nil {
229			return err
230		}
231	
232		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stdout, "paused session %s\n", s.ID)
233		return nil
234	}
235

input.content:
# Fix: `wasa session resume` retires the source session

## Context

Issue #122: `wasa session resume <id|branch>` mints a **new** running session via
`launch.CreateSession()` but never updates the **source** session's status. The
source stays `paused`, so it remains in the resumable pool and can be resumed
again and again — producing multiple running sessions per branch and duplicate
registry entries.

The TUI path (`launch.ResumeSession`) rebuilds in place and never duplicates, so
only the CLI path (`internal/cli/resume.go` → `sessionResume`) is affected.

The intended behavior: once a resume succeeds, the source transitions
`paused → exited` and disappears from the resumable pool, leaving exactly one
running session per branch. Lineage is already preserved via
`params.ResumedFrom = src.ID` (carried into the new session by `CreateSession`,
`internal/launch/launch.go:301`).

## Change

**File:** `internal/cli/resume.go`, function `sessionResume`.

After `launch.CreateSession` succeeds and **before** `reg.Save()`, retire the
source using the existing registry helper (`registry.go:214`, idempotent — a
no-op safe to call on an already-exited source, satisfying "resuming
already-exited sessions works without error"):

```go
s, err := launch.CreateSession(wasaHome(), reg, ws, params)
if err != nil {
    return err
}
reg.MarkExited(src.ID)          // retire the source; it leaves the resumable pool
if err := reg.Save(); err != nil {
    return err
}
```

Ordering matters: retire only after a successful create, so a failed launch
leaves the source paused and still resumable (early `return err` already does
this).

## Help text

Update `sessionResumeHelp` (top of `internal/cli/resume.go`) to document the
retirement. Extend the final paragraph, e.g.:

> The resumed session is a normal wasa session — registered, visible in the
> cockpit and recorded again, with resumedFrom set to the original session id.
> The source session is retired (marked exited) once the resume succeeds, so it
> no longer appears as resumable and only one running session exists per branch.
> The recorded branch must still exist; a deleted branch is a clear error and
> nothing is created.

## Verification

- `go build ./...` and `go test ./internal/cli/ ./internal/registry/ ./internal/launch/`.
- Existing `resume_test.go` (`resolveResumeTarget`) and `registry_test.go`
  (`TestMarkExited`, `TestMarkExitedDoesNotTouchLastUsedAt`) still cover the
  primitives; no new unit test is added because `sessionResume` drives real
  tmux via `launch.CreateSession` and isn't unit-testable in-package.
- Manual end-to-end check: create a session, `wasa session pause <id>`, then
  `wasa session resume <id>`. Confirm via `wasa session list --json` that the
  source is now `exited` (not `paused`), a single new `running` session exists
  on the branch, its `resumedFrom` points at the source, and a second
  `wasa session resume <same-id>` no longer treats it as resumable/duplicates it.
```
```
```

## Acceptance criteria mapping

- Source no longer resumable after resume → `MarkExited(src.ID)`.
- One running session per branch → source retired, no lingering paused twin.
- Resuming already-exited sessions works → `MarkExited` is idempotent.
- `resumedFrom` lineage preserved → unchanged `params.ResumedFrom` flow.
- Help text documents retirement → `sessionResumeHelp` update.

toolUseResult.content:
# Fix: `wasa session resume` retires the source session

## Context

Issue #122: `wasa session resume <id|branch>` mints a **new** running session via
`launch.CreateSession()` but never updates the **source** session's status. The
source stays `paused`, so it remains in the resumable pool and can be resumed
again and again — producing multiple running sessions per branch and duplicate
registry entries.

The TUI path (`launch.ResumeSession`) rebuilds in place and never duplicates, so
only the CLI path (`internal/cli/resume.go` → `sessionResume`) is affected.

The intended behavior: once a resume succeeds, the source transitions
`paused → exited` and disappears from the resumable pool, leaving exactly one
running session per branch. Lineage is already preserved via
`params.ResumedFrom = src.ID` (carried into the new session by `CreateSession`,
`internal/launch/launch.go:301`).

## Change

**File:** `internal/cli/resume.go`, function `sessionResume`.

After `launch.CreateSession` succeeds and **before** `reg.Save()`, retire the
source using the existing registry helper (`registry.go:214`, idempotent — a
no-op safe to call on an already-exited source, satisfying "resuming
already-exited sessions works without error"):

```go
s, err := launch.CreateSession(wasaHome(), reg, ws, params)
if err != nil {
    return err
}
reg.MarkExited(src.ID)          // retire the source; it leaves the resumable pool
if err := reg.Save(); err != nil {
    return err
}
```

Ordering matters: retire only after a successful create, so a failed launch
leaves the source paused and still resumable (early `return err` already does
this).

## Help text

Update `sessionResumeHelp` (top of `internal/cli/resume.go`) to document the
retirement. Extend the final paragraph, e.g.:

> The resumed session is a normal wasa session — registered, visible in the
> cockpit and recorded again, with resumedFrom set to the original session id.
> The source session is retired (marked exited) once the resume succeeds, so it
> no longer appears as resumable and only one running session exists per branch.
> The recorded branch must still exist; a deleted branch is a clear error and
> nothing is created.

## Verification

- `go build ./...` and `go test ./internal/cli/ ./internal/registry/ ./internal/launch/`.
- Existing `resume_test.go` (`resolveResumeTarget`) and `registry_test.go`
  (`TestMarkExited`, `TestMarkExitedDoesNotTouchLastUsedAt`) still cover the
  primitives; no new unit test is added because `sessionResume` drives real
  tmux via `launch.CreateSession` and isn't unit-testable in-package.
- Manual end-to-end check: create a session, `wasa session pause <id>`, then
  `wasa session resume <id>`. Confirm via `wasa session list --json` that the
  source is now `exited` (not `paused`), a single new `running` session exists
  on the branch, its `resumedFrom` points at the source, and a second
  `wasa session resume <same-id>` no longer treats it as resumable/duplicates it.
```
```
```

## Acceptance criteria mapping

- Source no longer resumable after resume → `MarkExited(src.ID)`.
- One running session per branch → source retired, no lingering paused twin.
- Resuming already-exited sessions works → `MarkExited` is idempotent.
- `resumedFrom` lineage preserved → unchanged `params.ResumedFrom` flow.
- Help text documents retirement → `sessionResumeHelp` update.

## Context

Issue #122: `wasa session resume <id|branch>` mints a **new** running session via
`launch.CreateSession()` but never updates the **source** session's status. The

again and again — producing multiple running sessions per branch and duplicate
registry entries.

The TUI path (`launch.ResumeSession`) rebuilds in place and never duplicates, so
only the CLI path (`internal/cli/resume.go` → `sessionResume`) is affected.

The intended behavior: once a resume succeeds, the source transitions
`paused → exited` and disappears from the resumable pool, leaving exactly one
running session per branch. Lineage is already preserved via
`params.ResumedFrom = src.ID` (carried into the new session by `CreateSession`,
`internal/launch/launch.go:301`).

## Change

**File:** `internal/cli/resume.go`, function `sessionResume`.

After `launch.CreateSession` succeeds and **before** `reg.Save()`, retire the
source using the existing registry helper (`registry.go:214`, idempotent — a
no-op safe to call on an already-exited source, satisfying "resuming
already-exited sessions works without error"):

```go

if err != nil {
    return err
}
reg.MarkExited(src.ID)          // retire the source; it leaves the resumable pool
if err := reg.Save(); err != nil {
    return err
}
```

Ordering matters: retire only after a successful create, so a failed launch
leaves the source paused and still resumable (early `return err` already does
this).

## Help text

Update `sessionResumeHelp` (top of `internal/cli/resume.go`) to document the
retirement. Extend the final paragraph, e.g.:

> The resumed session is a normal wasa session — registered, visible in the
> cockpit and recorded again, with resumedFrom set to the original session id.
> The source session is retired (marked exited) once the resume succeeds, so it
> no longer appears as resumable and only one running session exists per branch.
> The recorded branch must still exist; a deleted branch is a clear error and
> nothing is created.

## Verification

- `go build ./...` and `go test ./internal/cli/ ./internal/registry/ ./internal/launch/`.
- Existing `resume_test.go` (`resolveResumeTarget`) and `registry_test.go`
  (`TestMarkExited`, `TestMarkExitedDoesNotTouchLastUsedAt`) still cover the

  on the branch, its `resumedFrom` points at the source, and a second
  `wasa session resume <same-id>` no longer treats it as resumable/duplicates it.

## Acceptance criteria mapping

- Source no longer resumable after resume → `MarkExited(src.ID)`.
- One running session per branch → source retired, no lingering paused twin.
- Resuming already-exited sessions works → `MarkExited` is idempotent.
- `resumedFrom` lineage preserved → unchanged `params.ResumedFrom` flow.
- Help text documents retirement → `sessionResumeHelp` update.

content:
Build succeeds and all tests pass. The change is complete:

**`internal/cli/resume.go`** — two edits in the CLI resume path:
1. Added `reg.MarkExited(src.ID)` after `launch.CreateSession` succeeds and before `reg.Save()`, so the source transitions `paused → exited` and leaves the resumable pool. Placed after the create's error check, so a failed launch leaves the source paused and still resumable.
2. Extended `sessionResumeHelp` to document that the source is retired once resume succeeds.

This satisfies all acceptance criteria: the source is no longer resumable (`MarkExited`), exactly one running session exists per branch, `MarkExited` is idempotent so resuming an already-exited session works, `resumedFrom` lineage is untouched (still flows via `params.ResumedFrom`), and the help text documents the retirement.

2. Extended `sessionResumeHelp` to document that the source is retired once resume succeeds.

This satisfies all acceptance criteria: the source is no longer resumable (`MarkExited`), exactly one running session exists per branch, `MarkExited` is idempotent so resuming an already-exited session works, `resumedFrom` lineage is untouched (still flows via `params.ResumedFrom`), and the help text documents the retirement.

git add -A && git commit -q -m "fix(session): resume retires the source session

wasa session resume minted a new running session but left the source
paused, so it stayed in the resumable pool and could be resumed again,
producing duplicate sessions per branch. Mark the source exited once the
resume succeeds so only one running session exists per branch.

Closes #122" && git push -u origin HEAD 2>&1 | tail -5
